Now, welcome to Gene Get the Deal. Let's step back into the Wayback Machine and take a look at how we got here, right? Sometime ago you, you got a product or a service or a company or an idea that you wanted to sell. Something you wanted to pitch so you got in front of people to tell them about it. And one of two things happened, right? You tried some sales techniques that kind of define in the books like how to master the art of selling, right? Full of trial closes and you tried some double tie downs and gimmicky sales lines to parlay objections that the buyers came up with, right? You made us said things like, hey, you want it in red? If I could get it to you in red, would you take it today? Or you tried feel, felt, found, right? Well, Mr. Jones, I understand how you feel. I felt that way myself when I first encountered the color red, but I found after time it really grew on me and I learned to love it, right? So you tried all that nonsense gimmicky stuff and it didn't work and it made you feel uncomfortable. Those sales techniques made you feel sort of slimy and it didn't feel authentic and congruent and it was, it was disconnected from the real you. Yeah, the real you, right? That's it. You recall that somewhere along the way in your life and it was probably your mom said, be yourself. So after trying the sales route, which didn't feel good, you said, all right, I'll go the natural route to selling and pitching. So you sat down with a potential customer or a venture partner, whatever, and you, you just walked him through what you had. Why not? You're well spoken, funny, smart. You have a great product and company and there's real value in what you have to offer. A blind man in the dark could see the value you have to offer, right? So over the course of an hour, you explained the whole thing and, and doing this, you know, you did a pretty good job and eventually you saw a twinkle in their eye. You looked at the buyer and there's that shine. I had a tiger. But wait a second. Was that just the luminescence of the conference room light shining off their tears of boredom? Yes, it was. And you finally admitted yourself, hey, my pitch was a little unstructured and thinking back, uh, yeah, you did make it seem a little bit more complicated than it really is. So you decided, hey, I better get this pitch that I have, my sale pitch structured, right? And that's solid thinking right there. The better I pitch, the higher my close ratio will be. All right. So you started thinking a lot about better ways to present, persuade and promote. And, and by the intervention of the, the kind and merciful gods that are up there, you were introduced to pitch anything and the strong method. And here, and the answer to all our prayers, right, was pitch structure on steroids. And by the way, if this is the path you took to get here, it's the same path I took, right? Trying sales techniques and I found them uncomfortable and they made me feel slippery. And I wanted to, to wash myself off after using feel felt found and saying these crazy things they teach you in these sales books. And then I tried to be my own authentic self and that was not as good as it could be. So I started thinking about structure. So we took the same path to get here and now we're here talking about strong. And of course we start here with S as we have through the whole program. Every meeting, pitch and presentation is a social encounter governed by frames. Frames are a point of view, a perspective, a real position that you take. Frames don't combine or mix together. When your frame meets the frame of another person, they collide. They smash into each other. The strongest frame always wins. The stronger frame absorbs the weaker frame. If you have the dominant frame, the frame that breaks other frames and absorbs them, you are said to have frame control. And once you have frame control, people pay attention to you. So we learned to set the frame and how important framing is. And we got frames like the intrigue frame, the prize frame, the power frame and the moral authority frame and we learned to use them. From there, we moved on to tea or tell the story. This is where we gave the pitch, 20 minutes and we thought about it this way. When people make decisions, they lapse into an analytical mindset, which can be of course the greatest obstacle to closing a deal, this paradigmatic analytical mindset. So you have to grab the audience's attention with a narrative, a narrative arc. Create intrigue and keep their attention throughout the telling of your story. And during that story, you created an amazing character of yourself or maybe the other people in your business and your deal. You developed intrigue and you over 20 minutes really explained what's meaningful and you gave the big idea. You told the problem, your solution, how it works, the upside, the downside, the downside protection, the assumptions. All right, you gave your pitch. Then you moved on to understanding at the end of your pitch, you've had people listening to you for quite a long time. They're getting a little bit tired and actually you've told them most of what you have. So they don't just leave and get bored. It was time to reveal the intrigue. Because we say here it's easy for your audience to get distracted in the middle of your pitch. You got to get and keep their attention throughout by provocatively using the forces of tension, including risk, danger and uncertainty, as well as time constraint. Tension, intrigue, novelty, all these things to keep their attention. So we worked to reveal the intrigue. We did a good job there and then we realized it's time to really offer something. So we tried to understand how to offer the commercial terms of the deal, of what you're selling and still keep yourself as a prize. Still keep your status high and have they, the buyer, wanting the deal. Then we moved into N, recognizing that decisions are, of course, made on emotion. Everybody knows that. Not logic. And this is laying the final groundwork to get your audience fully immersed in your ideas. We learned about this concept of immersion and that if we under-tax people's perceptive systems and their cognitive systems, then they get apathetic and bored. And if we over-tax people's perception and people's cognitive systems, then they become stressed and afraid. And that immersion happens between those two states, under-taxed and over-taxed. We talked a lot about how to create this immersive experience where people are really satisfied and interested and closely paying attention and fully immersed in your experience. But beyond that, we then arrived here at G, getting the deal. The last few minutes of meeting are really the most critical in a way. Most of us, Acneedy at this point, if you do what's natural, comes natural to you, Acneedy. You must master the art of pulling away. I mean, G is much more than just pulling away as we're going to explore now. As you move into G, you realize you've now created the wanting from the buyer because you've achieved the hook point. At the hook point, the buyer is actually helping you close the deal. They're making things easier on you, and they are, they're immersed, they're into it, they're helping close the deal, they're leaning forward. It's clear they want what you have. But unfortunately, money has not transacted yet. It's one thing to get a verbal agreement, to get a signed purchase order in our business to get what we call a purchase and sale agreement. It's another thing altogether to actually get the money. As I'm very fond of saying, and if you do a deal with me, you'll hear this probably more times than you want to, there's many a slip between the cup and the lip. You know exactly what that means if you've been around in business more than a week. Things go wrong even when it looks like it's going to close and it's a layup. That's why we need to know how to get the deal. It's sure no one can tell you specifically what to say to induce a buyer to transfer the funds to you. There's too many variables across too many businesses and too many situations to give a punch list. Whether you sell espresso machines or all the way up to jet engines, every situation in business is different. But I think you'll see three major permutations out of the hundreds possible. You'll see three situations that happen most of the time. First situation is pretty obvious. The customer, the buyer, the partner loves your deal. The price you gave is good. The terms are good. They want to proceed to paperwork and really transfer the funds, give you the money. If you have a process at this point, obviously you put them in it immediately. Think of buying a car. Once you agree on the price, salesman goes away and you're just in a machine. The finance guy spits out the paperwork, asks if you want the extended warranty and there's no options at this point. You're just in a grinding machine of finance. Think about if you've ever done a legal settlement. The second you agree to it, boom, you're in a process and there's no more terms or discussion and you're just signing reams of paperwork that show you've agreed to the settlement. At the same way on Amazon.com, you click, buy it now. There's no more pricing. There's no more negotiation. You get one or two shipping options, but you're just in process. Once the buyer says, I want it, if you have a process, put them in it and get out of the way, get the money and you're closing. That's hopefully something you see all the time. The second scenario and this is more likely, the buyer will have one last gesticulation or last attempt at gaining a concession from you. This makes them feel good to exact a concession from you at the last minute. You probably do this yourself. Every deal goes through this step and I'm sure you're super familiar with it if you've sold anything. It usually goes like this. Because of some XYZ bullshit reason, we're ordering more than the average amount. Why don't you give us 5% off and we can just call it even and do this deal. Very common. We're the largest investors. We'll be the largest shareholders in the deal. Why don't you just give us a little bit of better share price, make it $2.05 rather than $2.25 and let's just call this a deal and sign it up. It's this request for a last minute concession. It's not much you can do about it. It's very common. You'll see it all the time. It's what you do with that request that's important. You need to know ahead of time. If you're going to concede, then concede with high status. Don't grumble about it and try and, if you know you're going to concede, don't let them know that you're unhappy about conceding or it's not really fair but you'll do it anyway. Do with high status. Just wave your hand and say, hey, whatever. If 5% discount means that much to you, it means that's what you need to close. Who cares? We focus on doing things right around here. Not grinding deals to the bone. If you want to do that, whatever. We'll close with it. Do it from a high status position. Don't whine about it. Lower your status. Or if you know that that kind of concession is not going to work for you, don't get into a back and forth grinding. Just commit and say, no, that is not going to happen. Make the period at the end of that sentence sound like the judge's gavel when he's issuing a death sentence. That's the decision. Remove the defendant from the courtroom. Sentence has been served. In the second scenario, you're going to see it's a request for a concession. You're going to see that all the time. Just know what you're going to do. You respond to that concession with high status and say, hey, whatever. We don't care if that's what you need. That's your problem. We don't grind. We're happy to do it. In the name of the deal, in the name of the relationship, let's just move on. That's what you need. We don't focus on minutia. Or if it doesn't work, be very firm. No. That is not going to happen. Those are the kinds of things you have to do is commit to some serious answers and commit to maintaining your status instead of whining through this concession request. The third most common scenario, you have correctly created wanton. You've gotten to the hook point. There are still small terms to negotiate. This is very common in my business in investments. Even though they want it and they said, we're going to invest in the deal, there's still things to negotiate, the closing date, the amount of the deposit, the breakup fees, what we call bad boy carve outs, what state arbitration will be done in, disclosure of third party fees. None of these should be deal breakers, but they're terms that just need to be worked through. It's very common and you just have to acknowledge that the deal is on its way to closing, but there's many terms to be worked through. You just have to plan to do it. Those are the three common scenarios. One, they want it. You have a process, throw them in the process, get them out of the way, let it close. Number two, they want a concession. Know how to deal with that concession, maintain your status. Or three, there's just going to be work to do as you're going to finalize the terms and get this deal closed. Whether it's any of the above situations, there are three main principles to consider. The first of these three principles is the importance of providing the buyer a sense of autonomy and self-determination. Sure, we have the best deal, the dominant product, the best service. You're the best salesperson or customer service. It's the natural option. There's no better competitor. I think we have to say, but of course, I'm biased. Yes, the value of the offer is there, but I can't, in the final analysis, Mr. Buyer, tell you what to do. Mr. Investor, I cannot tell you to an investor. I can tell you that I believe in it. I'm putting my own money in it. I'm very bullish on this deal. I've got deep due diligence. I've disclosed everything. It's a great project. I'm really in love with it myself, but I can't tell you what to do. You need to decide for yourself. So we have to give our buyers autonomy and self-determination. The second principle is, and we have talked about this many times, the destructive force of neediness. And here, maybe more than anywhere else in the pitch, neediness can really ruin the deal. And the third principle, we have to spend some time talking about this, is the need to commit to the things you're saying. This is a quick example. If you say, we only take 10 customers on a month, then that has got to be true. And if they are trying to add themselves as the 11th customer, you have to commit to that. If you've said it, you have to commit to those things that we're going to talk about, committing to the things that you've agreed to. First, let's go deeper into autonomy, self-determination. If you are nying, nagging, clying, or crowding your buyer, he's going to lose a sense of autonomy. If you limit, reduce, or take away your buyer's sense of self-determination at this point, at this point in the deal, then you're really courting disaster. The deal is going to blow up. Consider for a moment, why have people throughout history been willing to fight and even die for their freedom? Perhaps the answer is obvious. Oppression causes suffering, and we're all hardwired to flee suffering. But recent research suggests another reason. We also seem to be hardwired to desire autonomy. Autonomy can be defined as the ability to make choices according to one's own free will. If we feel coerced by external pressures like other people, our feeling of autonomy vanishes. Autonomy is the capacity of a rational individual to make an informed, uncoerced decision. It turns out that restrictions on our autonomy may lie at the heart of a great deal of our unhappiness. Studies show, for example, that one of the greatest sources of dissatisfaction among doctors isn't having to deal with insurance companies or paperwork, but lack of control over their daily schedules. If we are told by an authority figure, a person of high status, or even appear to do something, even if it is something we like, exercise, for example, we tend to resent it and will actually want to resist doing it in order to preserve a sense of autonomy. We always, in fact, have the power to say yes or no. And if that power is threatened in any way, we become fearful, resentful, and stressed. In deal making, only when the other person we are dealing with gives us the choice to say yes or no, do we feel a decision is our own to make and then we experience the pleasure of autonomy. The second principle to consider when getting the deal or working to get funds transferred to you, getting the deal fully closed, is this concept of neediness, right? Not only does neediness start to gnaw at the other person's sense of autonomy and self-determination, right? Because you're cloying and you're pressuring. That's what neediness is all about. Neediness is a primary trigger of fear in the brain of the buyer. I chose that word carefully. I didn't say in the mind of the buyer. I said it's a primary trigger of fear in the brain of the buyer. Because it's a physiological response. The human brain has not evolved much in the past 200,000 years. The brain AUG used to find food, shelter, and a mate. That is your brain. The emotions AUG used to get through the day are yours as well. AUG was constantly angry, hungry, or horny. These are your main emotional tools and they are only hiding behind a thin social layer. Your brain was developed in highly dangerous environments when all resources were limited. If you had something like shelter, food, water, you wanted to keep it because if you gave it to someone else, it was very hard to replace. As a result, your mind and your physiology are highly sensitive to scarcity. For example, if you go on a diet and restrict your intake of calories, you may actually become more fat. Why does this happen? When your body senses scarcity of food resources, it begins storing energy as fat. It believes a period of limited nutrition is coming and it wants to get ready to survive. When you want someone's money, a scarcity response is triggered in their brain. And that happens no matter how much they have or how good your intent is. Their crocodile brain interprets this in just one way that you want to take their resources. Since the primitive brain views all resources as scarce, your neediness is generating fear, stress, and fight response. The third principle to consider when really getting the deal or going to close and get the funds transferred to you is this concept of commitment. Now look, we've talked about many social tools in Pitch Mastery. Eradicating neediness is one. Neediness as a social tool. Time constraint. We know that time constraints in every situation cause weaker decision making by the buyer and the more likely that you're going to close the deal. Big is a great social tool. Tension, moral authority, right? These are all social tools. But we have not yet fully talked about the need to commit when we say these things, right? Let me explain. A time constraint is only a meaningful tool when you commit to it. If you say this deal is going to close on the 15th of the month and then on the 16th you call and say, hey, the deal's still open, would you like to invest in it? It doesn't work. We haven't committed. If you say along the way, hey, as I do, right, I'll only do a fair deal. The deal, yes, it has to work for you, but it has to work for me as well. And if it's not fair, then I'm going to blow out, take my toys, and go play in a different sandbox, willing to walk away, all right? Then if the deal becomes unfair and I still chase it, it shows I haven't committed to that statement. Have to commit to fairness if you say that's a decision tree for you. If you say, as I do many times, hey, if you keep pushing on this discount issue, this is not going to happen, right? This deal is going to stop and I'll walk, all right, if you keep pushing on this issue. So if you say that, but you don't do it, then you haven't committed. And now you've gone from strength to weakness. As I said before, you tell people you only take on 10 new customers a year, right? That means you only take on 10 new customers a year, unless there's some exceptional circumstance. If they come in two weeks late and say, hey, we want to be customer number 11, right, you have to hold to your commitment, your word, otherwise it doesn't mean anything. You have to commit to these things. And here's one way to think about it. Have you ever seen one of these cowboy movies where there's this really good looking 45-year-old family man? He's the sheriff. He's the baby saver. He's the anti-hero and the hero, right? He's riding his horse and he's just this amazing guy you want to get to know and be friends with and have it your side. He casts these guys amazing, right? And at some point he pulls aside the distracted young gunhand, the sidekick, and he looks him in the eye and he says, look son, you need to learn to say what you mean and mean what you say. What they're really talking about there is commitment. And when you see that kind of cowboy character say that thing, you know, if you're anything like me, you're inspired in that moment, it's hard coded into us to admire principles, morality, honesty, integrity. This is what compelling characters are made of in all the great movies that you care about, from Star Wars to Indiana Jones, you know, whatever it is. It is what inspires us about characters, their integrity, their morality. Characters, right? They go into tough situations and make difficult calls. They do the right thing when it's not easy. Keeping your word, doing what you say you'll do when it's not convenient anymore to do that thing, right? That's all character. But if you commit to the things you say, right, and you walk away at the point of the time constraint, or if you say, hey, I'm going to blow out of the deal if it's not fair, right? And then you actually do that. Or if you say, I'll walk away. If you continue to ask for price concessions and you then you actually walk away, right? You will lose deals. You will lose deals, right? Sometimes you tell someone, hey, you have to commit to be in this deal by Friday at noon, and they don't commit at Friday at noon, right? So on Monday, you have to tell them you're out. I'm going with another buyer. And they say, go ahead, go with the other buyer. We can't commit. You'll lose that deal, right? But so what? You kept your character. You kept your word. You did what you said you were going to do. You, you, you know, in the words of the cowboy, right? You said what you meant and you meant what you said. For every one of those guys you lose, for every deal you lose, you will win another five or 10 by being committed, right? Why is that? Because when you're doing things with commitment, when you're putting time constraints, putting time constraints and you're meeting that time constraint, when you say you won't give a pricing concession and then you don't give it, right? Why is it that these things work? Because because you're doing things exactly in phase with the basic nature of mankind, right? We want what we cannot have. We chase that which moves away from us. We chase that which moves away from us. We only value the things we pay for. So to get the deal, which means getting the funds transferred from their account into your account, give the buyer three things to do, right? Give the buyer autonomy and self-determination. Say, hey, I've taken you as far as I can take you. I've shown you the product, the upside, the downside protection, the value proposition, my commitment to it, why I think it's great. And I can only tell you about it. I can't decide for you. You have to decide for yourself, right? And then back away and let that buyer choose on his own. Get out of his way. That little bubble of safety will bring him in. You have to provide it. And if you don't, it's going to provide weakness. It's going to be difficult for you to get. There may be a verbal commitment, but it's going to be difficult for you to actually get the funds. The second thing we talked about, neediness. If there is ever a place in the pitch to eradicate neediness, it is here. The buyer is most hyper-vigilant to any neediness on your part. You want to not demonstrate any at this point. And the third thing is, of course, do what you say and say what you mean. Commit to your walkaway points, to your time constraints, to your pricing, and to your willingness to make concessions. Don't try and over-optimize deals. You will lose deals when you commit, but you'll gain many more. And lastly, it's worth saying it again. This all works because it's in phase with the basic human condition. We want what we cannot have. We chase that which moves away from us, and we only value the things we pay for.
